title: Quiz: Resource Estimation for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing
description: Test your understanding of resource estimation, qubit overhead, magic state factories, and space-time tradeoffs

questions.question: In surface code implementations, how does the number of physical qubits scale with code distance d?

options.text: O(d) - linear in distance
O(d^2) - quadratic in distance
O(d^3) - cubic in distance
O(2^d) - exponential in distance

questions.explanation: Surface codes require approximately 2d^2 physical qubits per logical qubit, where d is the code distance. This quadratic scaling is one reason why achieving high code distances (needed for low logical error rates) requires so many physical qubits.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of magic state factories in fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: To create entangled states between distant qubits
To produce high-fidelity magic states for implementing non-Clifford gates like T
To measure syndrome information during error correction
To initialize logical qubits in the |0> state

questions.explanation: Magic state factories distill noisy magic states into high-fidelity ones needed for T-gate implementation. Since T-gates cannot be implemented transversally on most codes (Eastin-Knill theorem), magic states are required, and factories are the dominant resource consumer in fault-tolerant systems.

questions.question: What is the space-time tradeoff in fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: Using more qubits always reduces execution time proportionally
Additional magic state factories can reduce T-gate execution time at the cost of more qubits
Higher code distances always reduce execution time
Time and space requirements are independent of each other

questions.explanation: The space-time tradeoff allows us to use more qubits (space) for additional magic state factories to produce T-states faster, reducing the bottleneck of T-gate execution time. However, this tradeoff is not always linear due to routing and scheduling constraints.

questions.question: Why is T-count (number of T-gates) such an important metric in fault-tolerant resource estimation?

options.text: T-gates are the only universal gates
T-gates require expensive magic state distillation while Clifford gates are cheap
T-gates have the highest error rates
T-gates cannot be parallelized

questions.explanation: Clifford gates (H, S, CNOT) can be implemented transversally at low cost, but T-gates require magic state injection from distillation factories. Magic state factories consume the majority of qubits in a fault-tolerant system, making T-count the primary cost metric.

questions.question: How does the logical error rate scale with code distance d for surface codes below threshold?

options.text: p_L ~ (p/p_th)^d - exponential in d
p_L ~ (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2) - exponential in half the distance
p_L ~ d * p - linear in d
p_L ~ p/d - inverse linear in d

questions.explanation: Below threshold (p < p_th), the logical error rate scales as p_L ~ (p/p_th)^((d+1)/2). This exponential suppression with distance is what makes error correction powerful, but it requires increasing physical resources (d^2 qubits) for each increment of d.

questions.question: In the 15-to-1 magic state distillation protocol, the output error scales as approximately 35 times the cube of the input error.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. The 15-to-1 protocol produces output error ~ 35*p^3 from input error p. This cubic suppression allows rapid improvement in fidelity: with input error 0.01, one round gives output error ~3.5*10^-5, and two rounds give ~1.5*10^-12.

questions.question: For factoring RSA-2048 using Shor's algorithm, what is the approximate order of magnitude for physical qubits needed?

options.text: Thousands of qubits
Tens of thousands of qubits
Millions of qubits
Billions of qubits

questions.explanation: Current estimates suggest factoring RSA-2048 requires approximately 20 million physical qubits with optimized implementations, or potentially more with less aggressive optimizations. This accounts for logical qubits, error correction overhead, and magic state factories.

questions.question: Which factors significantly affect the total physical qubit count in a fault-tolerant quantum computer? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Code distance required for target error rate
Number of logical qubits in the algorithm
Number of magic state factories for T-gate throughput
The color of the qubits

questions.explanation: Physical qubit count is determined by: (1) Code distance d, which sets qubits per logical qubit as ~2d^2, (2) Number of logical qubits needed by the algorithm, and (3) Magic state factories which often dominate total qubit count. The physical implementation details like 'color' are not relevant to qubit count estimates.
